0: <title>Environmental Consequences of Segregating Pig Urine and Feces - A Life Cycle Perspective </title>
Wageningen UR Livestock Research, Wageningen University and Research Centre, P.O. Box 135, Wageningen, 6700 AC the Netherlands, jerke.devries@wur.nl
Wageningen UR Livestock Research, Wageningen University and Research Centre
Animal Production Systems Group, Wageningen University
Written for presentation at the Ninth International Livestock Environment Symposium Sponsored by ASABE Valencia Conference Centre Valencia, Spain July 8 - 12, 2012

Abstract. The aim of this study was to assess the environmental consequences of segregating pig urine and feces under the animal house compared to conventional manure management. The environmental impact was assessed by using life cycle assessment. System boundaries encompassed the stages: in-house management, outside storage, transport and field application. Five impacts were evaluated: climate change (CC), terrestrial acidification (TA), marine eutrophication (ME), particulate matter formation (PMF), and fossil fuel depletion (FFD). Results showed a decrease for CC (up to 82%), TA, and PMF (up to 49%) when urine and feces were segregated, whereas ME increased (up to 11%). It was concluded that segregation of pig urine and feces reduces the environmental impact compared to a conventional manure management system and that the liquid state of the feces is essential for further improvement of the environmental performance of manure management with segregation.
Keywords. slurry management, ammonia emission, odor emission, livestock production, LCA.

The authors are solely responsible for the content of this technical presentation. The technical presentation does not necessarily reflect the official position of the American Society of Agricultural and Biological Engineers (ASABE), and its printing and distribution does not constitute an endorsement of views which may be expressed. St. Joseph, Mich.: ASABE. 2: <title>Environmental Consequences of Segregating Pig Urine and Feces - A Life Cycle Perspective </title>
Wageningen UR Livestock Research, Wageningen University and Research Centre, P.O. Box 135, Wageningen, 6700 AC the Netherlands, jerke.devries@wur.nl
Wageningen UR Livestock Research, Wageningen University and Research Centre
Animal Production Systems Group, Wageningen University
Written for presentation at the Ninth International Livestock Environment Symposium Sponsored by ASABE Valencia Conference Centre Valencia, Spain July 8 - 12, 2012

3: Gaseous emissions of methane (CH4) and ammonia (NH3), and to a lesser extent nitrous oxide (N2O) and nitrogen oxides (NOx), from in-house manure storage remain major contributors to the environmental impact in the life cycle of liquid animal manure (De Vries et al., 2012). These gaseous emissions contribute to environmental problems, such as climate change, terrestrial acidification, marine eutrophication and particulate matter formation. Additionally, emission of odor may result in nuisance leading to a negative perception of the livestock sector. Furthermore, emissions of nitrogenous gases do not only affect the environment directly, but also reduce the fertilizing capacity of the manure or its derived end products, hence reducing the potential to supply nutrients to crop production and substitute mineral fertilizer (Lopez-Ridaura et al., 2009).

Manure processing technologies developed for reducing gaseous emissions, and their related environmental impact, have focused mainly on secondary separation and processing, i.e. dealing with manure as mixture of urine and feces. The environmental impact of several processing technologies was assessed in life cycle assessment (LCA) studies (e.g. De Vries et al. (2012) and Lopez-Ridaura et al. (2009)). However, manure processing technologies which keep urine and feces separated (or segregated), directly after production have received only little attention.

Technologies that segregate urine and feces by using a belt system, e.g. the Kempfarm® system in the Netherlands, and filter nets have shown to reduce NH3 emissions up to 70%, CH4 emissions up to 80% and odor emission up to 74% as compared to conventional manure management systems (Aarnink et al., 2007; Lachance et al., 2005). Such technologies and their related environmental impact, however, have not been studied from an environmental life cycle perspective.

In this study we aim to assess the environmental reduction potential of segregating fattening pig urine and feces by a V-belt system (Kempfarm S. We compared this with the impact of conventional manure management, in which liquid manure is stored, transported, and field applied. We indicate critical management factors affecting the environmental performance and shifting of nitrogenous (N) emissions within and between environmental compartments and life cycle stages.

6: <header>2.1 Life cycle assessment approach and impact assessment </header>

We used LCA to compare the environmental impact of segregating pig urine and feces with the impact of conventional manure management. All environmental consequences as a result of changing manure management were included in the system boundary. This meant that for electricity supply and mineral fertilizer production we used so called marginal suppliers. The marginal suppliers were based on De Vries et al. (2012).

We evaluated five environmental impact categories: climate change (CC), terrestrial acidification (TA), marine eutrophication (ME), particulate matter formation (PMF) and fossil fuel depletion (FFD). Additionally, we included direct odor emissions from in-house storage of manure and urine and feces. The ReCiPe midpoint .1.04 impact assessment method was used in SimaPro v.7.3 (PRé Consultants, the Netherlands) to compare the environmental impacts.
7: <header>2.2 Functional unit and manure management scenarios </header>

A functional unit (FU) forms the basis for comparison as it quantifies the main function that a system provides. As we consider manure management from the point of excretion by the animal, we applied a FU of 1 ton liquid fattening pig manure or 1 ton segregated fattening pig urine and feces originating from the
same excreta. The same excreta ensured that equal amounts of nutrients and dry matter entered the system.

The reference scenario was based on a conventional manure management system of liquid manure, including in-house storage under a slatted floor (60% of pen area), outside storage in a covered concrete tank, transport and field application (Fig. 1). Subsequently, the manure was applied on arable land using a manure injector, where it was assumed to substitute mineral N, P, and K-fertilizer.

"Reference system"|"Animal liquid housing & storage (4 manure months)"|"1 ton liquid manure"|"Outside manure storage (2 months)"||"(31 km) Transport"||"Manure application (injection)"|||"Avoided mineral N, P, K-fertilizer" "Reference system"|"Animal liquid housing & storage (4 manure months)"|"1 ton liquid manure"|"Outside manure storage (2 months)"||"(31 km) Transport"||"Manure application (injection)"|||"Avoided mineral N, P, K-fertilizer" |||||||||| "Urine and feces segregation"|"Animal housing of urine & segregation and feces"|0.52 ton urine|Outside storage (urine, 6 months)||Transport (31 km)||Urine application (injection)|||"Avoided mineral N, P, K-fertilizer" "Urine and feces segregation"|"Animal housing of urine & segregation and feces"|0.48 ton feces|Outside storage (feces, 6 months)||Transport (31 km)||(spreading/injection) Feces application|(spreading/injection) Feces application||"Avoided mineral N, P, K-fertilizer"
Figure 1: Reference system for manure management and the system with urine and feces segregation. The dotted boxes represent avoided mineral fertilizer production.

The scenario with segregation of urine and feces included in-house segregation, outside storage, transport and field application (Fig. 1). Segregation was obtained by a V-shaped belt under a slatted concrete floor. Urine flowed down constantly, firstly to the middle of the belt, and secondly to the end of the belt into a collection pit. Feces were removed daily by rotating the belt. Feces management was dependent on its liquid state. The liquidity of the feces varied between solid and liquid depending mainly on the separation efficiency and on the use of bedding material. As baseline scenario, it was considered that solid feces (dry matter > 20%) were stored aerobically in an open shed, whereas in a second scenario liquid feces (dry matter 20%) were stored anaerobically in a covered concrete tank. Field application occurred by a manure injector (urine and liquid feces) and a solid manure spreader with incorporation into the soil directly after spreading (solid feces).

10: <header>2.3 Products chemical composition and emission data </header>

Chemical compositions and excretion rates of urine and feces in the reference scenario were based on average data from the Dutch fattening pig sector. Average excretion rates and the composition of urine and feces in the case of segregation were based on Aarnink et al. (2007) and then scaled to the composition of the manure in the reference scenario to obtain equal N and P amounts per ton of segregated urine and feces.

During storage, segregation and field application NH3, N2O, N2, NOx, CH4, and NO3 are emitted and also odor (Table 1). Emissions during and after application of urine and feces were based on several sources used in De Vries et al. (2012) and assumed to be similar to those of respectively de-watered liquid fraction (mineral concentrate) and solid fraction from separated liquid manure (Table 1).
Odor emission during outside storage and application of the products was not considered due to lack of data.

Background emissions are emissions not directly occurring from the considered system, but from supporting systems. Emissions from background systems considered were: electricity production, transportation of products, mineral fertilizer production and capital goods production. All environmental impact data were taken from the Ecoinvent database (EcoinventCentre, 2007).
Table 1: Applied emission factors, energy consumption and fertilizer replacement values in the respective life cycle stages of the reference scenario and scenarios with segregation.

Ref = reference scenario, Seg = segregation scenario, OU = odor units, NFRVV = nitrogen fertilizer replacement value, TAN = total ammoniacal nitrogen, n.d. = no data. Data between brackets represent estimated minimum and maximum values or the relative range of uncertainty. a VROM (2012). b Mosquera and Hol (2012). C Aarnink et al. (2007). d Groenestein et al. (2011). e Modeled values based on De Vries et al. (2012). f Several sources in De Vries et al. (2012). g Dekker et al. (2009). h EcoinventCentre (2007).

(2011). e Modeled values based on De Vries et al. (2012). f Several sources in De Vries et al. (2012). g Dekker et al. (2009). h EcoinventCentre (2007).
14: <header>3.1 Impact of segregation with solid feces </header>

CC reduced with 66% in the scenario with segregation and solid feces compared to the reference scenario (Fig. 2). This reduction was due to lower CH4 emission during in-house segregation as anaerobic conditions were avoided compared to liquid manure storage in the reference scenario. During storage of solid feces, emission of N2O, however, was considerably higher compared to outside storage of liquid manure (57 kg CO2-eq for solid feces compared to 0.83 kg CO2-eq for liquid manure). Moreover, N2O emission from urine application increased total N2O emission compared to liquid manure in the reference scenario. The increase of N2O emission during storage and application, however, did not counteract the reduction in CH4 emission. This led to an overall net reduction of GHGs.

TA and PMF reduced with respectively 29% and 22% in the scenario with segregation compared to the reference scenario (Fig. 2). This was mainly due to less NH3 emission during storage, although NO emission during storage and NH3 emissions during application increased.
ME increased 11% in the scenario with segregation compared to the reference scenario. This was due to increased NO emission from storage of solid feces.
FFD hardly changed compared to the reference scenario due to similar amounts of avoided fertilizer.
Figure 2: Relative environmental impact of the reference (liquid manure based system) and two systems with segregation of urine and feces (solid/ stackable and liquid feces).
15: <header>3.1 Impact of segregation with solid feces </header>

CC reduced with 66% in the scenario with segregation and solid feces compared to the reference scenario (Fig. 2). This reduction was due to lower CH4 emission during in-house segregation as anaerobic conditions were avoided compared to liquid manure storage in the reference scenario. During storage of solid feces, emission of N2O, however, was considerably higher compared to outside storage of liquid manure (57 kg CO2-eq for solid feces compared to 0.83 kg CO2-eq for liquid manure). Moreover, N2O emission from urine application increased total N2O emission compared to liquid manure in the reference scenario. The increase of N2O emission during storage and application, however, did not counteract the reduction in CH4 emission. This led to an overall net reduction of GHGs.

TA and PMF reduced with respectively 29% and 22% in the scenario with segregation compared to the reference scenario (Fig. 2). This was mainly due to less NH3 emission during storage, although NO emission during storage and NH3 emissions during application increased.
ME increased 11% in the scenario with segregation compared to the reference scenario. This was due to increased NO emission from storage of solid feces.
FFD hardly changed compared to the reference scenario due to similar amounts of avoided fertilizer.
Figure 2: Relative environmental impact of the reference (liquid manure based system) and two systems with segregation of urine and feces (solid/ stackable and liquid feces).
16: <header>3.2 Impact of segregation with liquid feces </header>

CC reduced 82% in the scenario with segregation and liquid feces compared to the reference scenario (Fig. 2). This was due to the same causes as in the case of segregation with solid feces. However, an additional decrease was obtained due to lower N2O emission from storage of liquid feces, instead of solid feces, although CH4 emissions were slightly higher.

TA and PMF reduced 49% in the scenario with segregation compared to the reference scenario. Causes were similar to the case of segregation with solid feces. The additional decrease was due to lower NOx emission during storage of the liquid feces and lower NH3 emission during field application, as liquid feces were now injected compared to surface spreading of solid feces.

ME increased 9% with segregation compared to the reference scenario. This was mainly related to higher NO3 emission as a result of more N retained in the feces fraction although NO and NH3 emissions were lower. This indicates that shifting within and between environmental compartments occurred.
FFD varied only very little (4%), although feces were now injected instead of spread onto the field.
17: An important consideration of this study was to include the full life cycle of the segregation system in order to reveal shifting of N emissions within and between environmental compartments and life cycle stages. It was shown that N was emitted in different compounds depending on circumstances, such as manure type and storage system. Furthermore, these compounds contribute to various environmental compartments or are emitted in different stages of the life cycle. In concrete, for TA, PMF, and ME, NH3 and NO emissions altered within and between the impact categories. Moreover, emissions of N in the reference scenario and the scenarios with segregation changed between the life cycle stages. In the case of segregation more N was retained in the urine and feces in an earlier stage of the life cycle leading to less emission, but increasing the risk of emission in latter stages. This was highlighted mostly in the segregation scenario with liquid feces. Additionally, within CC, N2O and CH4 from storage varied depending on the oxygen maintained in the storage environment. It should be emphasized that both gases contribute in a considerably different extent to CC (a multiplier of 298 for N2O and 25 for CH4). Overall, this illustrates the strength of LCAs and shows the importance to realize these interactions and hence to include accompanying emission factors related to the circumstances to be covered.

From an environmental perspective it was found that a critical management factor in the scenarios with segregation was the liquid state and related management of feces. It would be preferable to avoid solid feces as an end product due to potential N2O and NO emissions from storage and NH3 emission from application. A promising alternative would be to anaerobically (co)-digest this fraction reducing the need of storage in advance and resulting in the production of bio-energy (De Vries et al., 2012). Other alternatives include composting or drying. However, such technologies have shown to increase the emissions of NH3 and N2O leading to an increased environmental impact (Lopez-Ridaura et al., 2009).

Segregation reduces in-house odor emission compared to conventional manure storage and may result in less nuisance in residential areas, as critical threshold values will not be exceeded as often than with conventional manure management. Odor impacts, however, remain strongly dependent on local conditions of climate and the location of the farm (VROM, 2012).

It was shown that N was emitted in different compounds depending on circumstances, such as manure type and storage system. 19: Segregation of pig urine and feces reduced climate change, terrestrial acidification, and particulate matter formation compared to conventional manure management. Marine eutrophication increased as a result of segregation whereas fossil fuel depletion did not change. A critical management factor found was the liquidity and related management of feces. Further processing of solid feces by means of e.g., anaerobic digestion, would be preferable from an environmental perspective. Moreover, shifting of nitrogenous emissions within and between environmental impact categories and life cycle stages was highlighted. The results strongly support that segregation of pig urine and feces improves the environmental performance of manure management with regard to climate change, terrestrial acidification and particulate matter formation.
